title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an athlete's experience of preparing for and competing in a long-distance ultramarathon. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Beyond the Finish Line
content.subtitle: A sports magazine article about the mental and physical demands of ultramarathon running.
content.text: The Atacama Crossing is 250 kilometres long, runs through one of the driest places on earth, and must be completed in six stages over seven days. Competitors carry everything they need to survive — food, sleeping gear, emergency equipment — in a pack that begins the race weighing approximately ten kilograms. When I signed up, I told people it was because I wanted to test my limits. [31] Looking back now, I think that explanation was a convenient simplification of something I didn't fully understand about myself at the time. [/31] || Training for an ultramarathon is a peculiar exercise in sustained discomfort. The objective is not to become comfortable, which is impossible, but to expand your tolerance of being uncomfortable. Over eighteen months, my weekly mileage grew steadily, my kit was refined through a series of experiments and failures, and I developed what I can only describe as a professional relationship with blisters. [32] The physical adaptation, though considerable, turned out to be the easier half of the preparation. [/32] What no training plan fully prepares you for is the psychological dimension — the part of the race that occurs entirely inside your own head. || By day three of the crossing, I had developed a technique I thought of as 'shrinking the world'. Faced with fifty kilometres of salt flat still ahead of me, I refused to think about the distance. Instead, I focused exclusively on reaching the next marker pole — a target perhaps three hundred metres away. [33] It was a technique several experienced ultra-runners had described to me, but understanding it intellectually and actually experiencing its effect were entirely different things. [/33] When it worked, time seemed to compress. When it didn't, the distance stretched in every direction like a landscape in a bad dream. || The sixth stage nearly ended my race. A combination of exhaustion, a navigational error, and rapidly falling temperature in the late afternoon left me sitting on the desert floor, unable to identify a compelling reason to stand up again. [34] What eventually moved me was not willpower, in the heroic sense that sports writing tends to favour, but something considerably less dramatic: the thought of the other competitors who were still moving, and the mild social embarrassment of not finishing. [/34] I find it more honest to admit this than to construct a more satisfying narrative. || I crossed the finish line on the afternoon of the seventh day, in forty-second place out of over two hundred and fifty entrants. In competitive terms, this was unremarkable. [35] In my own terms — and I have come to think that ultramarathon running is fundamentally a private conversation between a person and their own capacities — it was everything I had come for. [/35] || I have since been asked, many times, whether I would do it again. My answer has remained consistent: I don't know. [36] What I do know is that the version of myself who finished the race is different from the one who started it, and that the difference has nothing to do with fitness. [/36]

questions.question: The writer suggests that their original reason for entering the race
options: A) was a complete lie designed to impress other people.
B) did not fully capture the real, less clearly understood motivation.
C) was based on advice from other ultramarathon competitors.
D) showed how little they knew about the demands of the sport.
questions.explanation: The writer says the explanation was 'a convenient simplification of something I didn't fully understand about myself', suggesting the true motivation was more complex and personal, not a lie.

questions.question: The phrase 'professional relationship with blisters' suggests that the writer
options: A) had taken expert advice on how to prevent foot injuries during training.
B) had come to accept minor injury as a normal and manageable part of training.
C) felt that foot care was the most important element of physical preparation.
D) was proud of enduring more physical pain than most athletes.
questions.explanation: A 'professional relationship' implies detached acceptance rather than distress — the writer treats blisters as routine, not remarkable, reflecting tolerance for discomfort developed through training.

questions.question: What does the writer mean when they say that understanding and experiencing the technique were 'entirely different things'?
options: A) The technique was harder to learn in practice than experienced runners had suggested.
B) Knowing about the strategy in theory did not convey what it actually felt like to use it.
C) The technique worked differently for the writer than it did for other runners.
D) Advice from experienced runners was not relevant to the conditions in the Atacama.
questions.explanation: The contrast is between intellectual understanding (having been told about the technique) and lived experience (feeling its effect for yourself) — a distinction between knowledge and experience.

questions.question: The writer chooses to describe what kept them going in order to
options: A) criticise the way sport is usually portrayed in the media.
B) show that their motivation was less admirable than expected.
C) offer an honest account rather than a more dramatic but false one.
D) argue that social pressure is a more powerful motivator than determination.
questions.explanation: The writer explicitly prefers honesty ('I find it more honest to admit this') over 'constructing a more satisfying narrative', valuing accuracy over a flattering portrayal of their motivation.

questions.question: The writer describes ultramarathon running as 'a private conversation' to emphasise that
options: A) competitors should not discuss their race strategies with each other.
B) the real significance of the race lies in what it means to the individual runner.
C) the race is better enjoyed when run alone rather than with a group.
D) competitive rankings are the least important aspect of the sport.
questions.explanation: The 'private conversation' metaphor suggests the experience is internal and personal, and the sentence continues 'between a person and their own capacities' — confirming the personal, not competitive, significance.

questions.question: The writer's final reflection suggests that the most significant outcome of the race was
options: A) an improvement in their physical fitness that will last for years.
B) a personal change that cannot be explained by physical achievement alone.
C) the realisation that they want to compete in more extreme events.
D) a greater understanding of why other people choose to run ultramarathons.
questions.explanation: The writer states the difference in themselves 'has nothing to do with fitness', pointing to an inner, personal transformation rather than a physical or competitive one.
